default_headers.technical: Returns a CaseInsensitiveDict containing four HTTP headers: User-Agent (populated dynamically via `default_user_agent()`), Accept-Encoding (set to a predefined constant), Accept (set to `*/*`), and Connection (set to `keep-alive`). The CaseInsensitiveDict wrapper allows header keys to be accessed regardless of case sensitivity, which is important since HTTP headers are case-insensitive by specification. No side effects or external state modifications occur.

parse_header_links.human: This function parses HTTP Link headers (used in web responses to indicate related resources) and extracts structured information from them. It breaks down complex link header strings into individual links with their URLs and attributes (like "rel" and "type"), returning a list of dictionaries that are easier to work with programmatically.
parse_header_links.technical: The function strips whitespace and quotes from the input value, then splits on comma-delimited `<` patterns to isolate individual links. For each link, it separates the URL from parameters using semicolon as delimiter, strips formatting characters, and parses key-value pairs from the parameters section. Returns a list of dictionaries where each dictionary contains "url" and attribute keys (rel, type, etc.) extracted via string splitting and stripping operations.

guess_json_utf.human: This function detects which character encoding (UTF-8, UTF-16, or UTF-32) was used to encode JSON data by examining the first few bytes. JSON files always start with recognizable ASCII characters, so the pattern of null bytes reveals the encoding. The function also checks for byte-order marks (BOM) that explicitly indicate the encoding format.
guess_json_utf.technical: Inspects the first 4 bytes of data to identify UTF encoding by: (1) checking for BOM signatures using `codecs.BOM_*` constants, (2) counting null bytes via `sample.count(_null)`, and (3) analyzing null-byte patterns at specific positions (every other byte or consecutive bytes) to distinguish between UTF-16 and UTF-32 variants (big-endian vs. little-endian). Returns the detected encoding name as a string, or `None` if detection fails.

prepend_scheme_if_needed.human: This function ensures a URL has a valid protocol (scheme) at the beginning, adding one if it's missing. It handles edge cases where URLs might be malformed or missing parts, reconstructing them properly so they can be used reliably.
prepend_scheme_if_needed.technical: Parses the input URL using `parse_url()` to extract components (scheme, auth, host, port, path, query, fragment). Reconstructs auth into netloc if present, assigns `new_scheme` if scheme is None, ensures path is a string, then returns the reassembled URL via `urlunparse()`. check_header_validity.human: This function validates that an HTTP header (consisting of a name and value) follows proper formatting rules. _validate_header_part.technical: Selects a regex validator from `_HEADER_VALIDATORS_STR` or `_HEADER_VALIDATORS_BYTE` based on whether `header_part` is `str` or `bytes`, then calls `validator.match()` to validate the format. urldefragauth.human: This function cleans up a URL by removing two things: the fragment (the part after #) and any authentication credentials (username/password before the @). rewind_body.human: This function resets the position of a file-like request body back to where it started, allowing it to be read again when an HTTP request is redirected. This is necessary because when a redirect occurs, the request body may need to be resent, but the file pointer has already moved forward. If the body can't be rewound, an error is raised to inform the caller.
rewind_body.technical: The function retrieves the `seek` method from `prepared_request.body` and checks if both the method exists and `_body_position` is a valid integer type. proxy_bypass_registry.human: This function checks whether a given hostname should bypass the system proxy by consulting Windows Registry settings. Parses ProxyOverride as a semicolon-separated list, then iterates through each rule, converting glob patterns (* and ?) to regex equivalents. Matches the input host against regex patterns using case-insensitive comparison (re.I flag). Returns True on first match or if host has no dots and rule is "<local>"; returns False if registry access fails or no rules match.

proxy_bypass.human: This function determines whether a specific host should bypass proxy settings. src/requests/cookies.py.human: This module provides a user-friendly cookie jar implementation that makes working with HTTP cookies in Python feel like using a regular dictionary. src/requests/exceptions.py.human: This module defines the exception hierarchy for the requests library, providing specialized error types for different failure scenarios that can occur when making HTTP requests. It handles JSON parsing errors, network connectivity issues, SSL/TLS problems, timeouts, redirect loops, malformed URLs, and other HTTP-related failures. src/requests/exceptions.py.technical: Defines a hierarchical exception system with RequestException as the base class, branching into specialized categories: connection/network errors (ConnectionError, ProxyError, SSLError, Timeout variants), HTTP protocol errors (HTTPError, TooManyRedirects, ChunkedEncodingError), URL/schema validation errors (URLRequired, InvalidSchema, InvalidURL, InvalidHeader), JSON handling errors (InvalidJSONError, JSONDecodeError), and stream/body errors (StreamConsumedError, UnrewindableBodyError, RetryError). src/requests/help.py.technical: The module provides three functions that work together to collect and display diagnostic metadata: `_implementation()` detects the Python runtime variant (CPython, PyPy, Jython, IronPython) and retrieves its version; `info()` aggregates system diagnostics by querying `platform` module details and extracting version attributes from dependencies (urllib3, cryptography, idna, charset_normalizer, chardet, PyOpenSSL) using `getattr()` with graceful None defaults for optional packages; `main()` serves as the CLI entry point that calls `info()` and outputs the result as formatted JSON. This design integrates with requests' request/response lifecycle to support event listeners at various stages without coupling components directly.

It ensures consistent behavior across different Python environments.
src/requests/packages.py.technical: Imports `sys` and `compat` module to conditionally manage package namespace handling and Python version compatibility. Likely defines package aliases or imports that redirect to vendored library locations, manipulating `sys.modules` to control how dependencies are resolved. Acts as a packaging abstraction layer that decouples requests from external package managers and enables vendored dependency isolation within the requests namespace.

src/requests/sessions.py.human: This module manages HTTP sessions—persistent connections that remember settings across multiple requests. It handles redirects by automatically following chains of server redirects to final destinations, manages authentication credentials securely (stripping them when redirecting to untrusted hosts), merges session-level defaults with per-request settings, and orchestrates the entire request-response lifecycle including cookie management, proxy configuration, and response hooks. Essentially, it provides a convenient, stateful way to make multiple HTTP requests while automatically handling common scenarios like redirects and credential management.
src/requests/sessions.py.technical: Implements two main classes: `SessionRedirectMixin` (handles redirect logic via `resolve_redirects()`, `rebuild_auth()`, `rebuild_proxies()`, `rebuild_method()`) and `Session` (manages request state and lifecycle). Session stores default headers, cookies, proxies, auth, and adapters; provides HTTP verb convenience methods (GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE, etc.) that delegate to `request()`. Core orchestration happens in `send()` which executes PreparedRequest objects through appropriate adapters, follows redirects, and manages cookies. Uses `merge_setting()` and `merge_hooks()` for intelligent configuration merging, `get_adapter()` for protocol-based routing, and supports serialization via `__getstate__()/__setstate__()`. Integrates with adapters, auth handlers, and cookie jars as dependencies.

src/requests/status_codes.py.human: This module provides a convenient, user-friendly interface for accessing HTTP status codes in the requests library. It dynamically maps status code numbers (like 200, 404, 500) to their human-readable names (like "OK", "Not Found", "Internal Server Error") and makes them accessible as module attributes in both lowercase and uppercase formats. It also auto-generates comprehensive documentation listing all available status codes, making it easy for developers to reference what each code means without memorizing numbers.
src/requests/status_codes.py.technical: The module uses dynamic attribute injection via `setattr()` to populate itself with status code mappings from a `_codes` dictionary that pairs numeric codes with their associated names. It creates dual-case variants (lowercase and uppercase) of each code name for flexible access patterns. The `doc()` function provides a documentation lookup API that formats code-to-names mappings into readable strings. Module initialization automatically generates and appends formatted reference documentation to `__doc__`, leveraging the structures dependency for organizing the underlying code-to-names data structure. This design enables attribute-style access (e.g., `codes.ok`, `codes.NOT_FOUND`) rather than dictionary lookups.

src/requests/structures.py.human: This module provides utility dictionary classes that make working with HTTP headers and similar data more convenient. The main problem it solves is that HTTP headers are case-insensitive (e.g., "Content-Type" and "content-type" should be treated identically), but Python dictionaries are case-sensitive by default. It offers a CaseInsensitiveDict class that allows you to access values regardless of key capitalization, and a LookupDict for named lookups, making the requests library easier to use when dealing with headers and other case-insensitive data.
src/requests/structures.py.technical: The module provides two specialized dictionary classes: CaseInsensitiveDict, which stores key-value pairs with lowercase-normalized keys while preserving original key casing, and LookupDict, a simple wrapper for named attribute access. CaseInsensitiveDict implements the Mapping protocol (__getitem__, __setitem__, __eq__) using an internal _store dictionary that maps lowercased keys to (original_key, value) tuples. This design enables case-insensitive lookups and comparisons while maintaining the original key format for display purposes. The classes integrate with the requests library's header handling and configuration management systems.

src/requests/utils.py.human: The `src/requests/utils.py` module is a comprehensive utility library that prepares data for HTTP communication. It transforms and normalizes various input formats (dictionaries, lists, files, headers) into standardized structures, handles character encoding and decoding for HTTP content, validates and extracts network information (IP addresses, proxy settings), and manages HTTP header formatting. These utilities abstract away common data handling concerns so that higher-level request functions can focus on actual network communication without worrying about format inconsistencies or encoding issues.
src/requests/utils.py.technical: This module provides 43 utility functions organized around three core responsibilities: (1) data transformation functions that normalize diverse input formats and handle encoding/decoding with codecs and io libraries, (2) network validation functions that parse IP addresses, CIDR notation, and proxy configurations using socket and struct modules, and (3) HTTP infrastructure functions that parse URLs, manage headers, detect character encodings, and determine proxy bypass logic. The design leverages Python's standard library (contextlib, tempfile, re, sys) to create a lightweight dependency layer that other requests modules build upon for consistent, validated HTTP communication.

repo_summary.human: The requests library is a user-friendly HTTP client for Python that simplifies making web requests and interacting with APIs. It abstracts away the complexity of low-level networking, allowing developers to easily send GET/POST requests, handle authentication, manage cookies, and work with HTTPS connections using intuitive, Pythonic syntax. The library handles common scenarios automatically—like following redirects, validating SSL certificates, detecting character encodings, and managing persistent connections—so developers can focus on building applications rather than dealing with HTTP protocol details. It's designed for anyone building Python applications that need to communicate with web services, REST APIs, or other HTTP-based resources.
repo_summary.technical: The requests library is a layered HTTP client built on urllib3 that provides connection pooling, SSL/TLS support, and automatic retry logic. Core architecture consists of: Sessions (manage persistent state, defaults, cookies, and authentication across requests), Adapters (pluggable transport handlers that route requests to appropriate protocols via urllib3's PoolManager), PreparedRequest/Response objects (manage the request/response lifecycle with encoding/decoding), and a hook system (event-driven callbacks for extensibility). Supporting subsystems include CaseInsensitiveDict for HTTP headers, RequestsCookieJar for cookie management, specialized exception hierarchy for error handling, and comprehensive utilities for URL parsing, encoding detection, proxy configuration, and authentication (Basic, Digest, and Proxy auth schemes). All modules integrate through a unified Session interface that orchestrates redirects, credential management, and protocol-appropriate routing.
